macro_segments.title: Responsibility for Mezuzah in Rental Properties

micro_segments.title: The Baraita Rule: Tenant Places Mezuzah, Leaves It Upon Departure Unless Replacing a Non-Jew
Tragic Case of Improper Mezuzah Removal and Rabbinic Interpretation
Distinction Between Eretz Yisrael and Chutz La'Aretz; Practical Application in Modern Rentals
Reasoning for Leaving Mezuzah: Tosafot on Demons, Ritva on Divine Presence
Contemporary Uncertainty About Next Tenant and Practical Halakhic Conclusions
Linguistic Analysis: Why 'Nochri' Remained Uncensored in This Context
Multi-Unit Buildings: Landlord Responsibility When Multiple Tenants Occupy Same Structure
Rashi's Explanation: Mezuzah Obligation Based on Who Passes Through the Doorway

macro_segments.title: Manure and Ash Rights from Ovens and Courtyards in Rental Agreements

micro_segments.title: Mishna Clause: Owner Retains Manure; Tenant Gets Only Ashes from Oven and Stove
Case Analysis: Courtyard Not Rented to Tenant; What Happens When Foreign Animals Deposit Manure
Rabbi Yosi b'Rabbi Chanina's Principle: Courtyard Acquires Ownerless Items Without Owner's Knowledge
Challenge to R. Yosi: Lost Objects Declared as Future Acquisitions by Courtyard Proclamation
Distinction: Principle Applies Only to Fenced and Guarded Courtyard (Chatzer Hamishtameret)
Psychological Fence: Town Gossip About Lost Items Prevents Others from Retrieving Them
Third Challenge: Manure Grabbed from Air Belongs to Tenant, Not Owner—Testing R. Yosi's Principle
Abaye's Resolution: Container Catches Manure Directly; Never Passes Through Air
Rava's Disagreement: Something Interrupted in Midair Cannot Be Considered Resting in Courtyard
Debate on Airspace: Does Airspace Belong to Courtyard Owner (Klutah K'Mi Shehunchah Damya)
Example: Wallet Thrown Through One Door and Exiting Another—What Is Acquired When?
Things Remaining in Stable or Courtyard Belong to Owner; Distinction from Portable Items
Rav Ashi: Renting Out Courtyard Generally Excludes the Stable (Refet) as Separate Premises
Dovecote Challenge: Doves of Dovecotes Obligate Shiluach Haken; Do They Become Acquired Without Knowledge?
Rava's Solution: Egg Obligation (Shiluach) Begins at Majority Emergence Before Landing; Kinyan Chatzer Requires Landing
Prohibition of Taking Eggs While Mother Sits: Cannot Acquire Eggs Until Mother Sent Away (Shelo Da'ato)
Principle Parallel to Shlichut: Courtyard as Agent Can Only Acquire What Owner Himself Could Acquire
Resolution: Prohibition Against Taking Eggs Only M'deRabbanan Due to Darchei Shalom, Not True Theft

macro_segments.title: Leap Year and Ambiguous Rental Agreements: Determining Responsibility for Thirteenth Month

micro_segments.title: Mishna Rule: Year-Long Rental Includes Leap Month for Tenant; Month-by-Month Rental Benefits Landlord
Case in Tzippori: Bathhouse Rental for Twelve Gold Coins Per Year, Stated as One Per Month
Ruling by Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel and Rabbi Yosi: Split the Thirteenth Month's Rent
Gemara Challenge: This Case Appears to Contradict the Simple Rule; Correction Using 'Chasorei Mechasra'
Rav's Opinion: If He Had Been Consulted, Entire Month Would Go to Landlord (Following Property Chazaka)
Rav's Principle: Tfus Lashon Acharon—Follow the Last Clause of Ambiguous Agreements
Shmuel's Middle Position: Split Depends on When Landlord Arrives to Collect Rent During the Month
Shmuel's Three Scenarios: Beginning of Month (All to Landlord), Middle (Split), End (All to Tenant)
Rav Nachman's Opinion: Land Always in Possession of Owner; Property Chazaka Determines Entire Claim
Reconciliation: Tfus Lashon Acharon Applies According to Rav; Would Reverse If Clauses Were Reversed

macro_segments.title: Burden of Proof in Disputed Wage and Rent Payments: Timing and Chazaka

micro_segments.title: Case: Tenant Claims Payment, Landlord Denies Receipt—Who Bears Burden of Proof?
Distinction: Before Deadline Passes (Assumption: Not Paid); After Deadline (Assumption: Paid)
Parallel from Bechorot: Father's Death Before/After 30 Days Changes Assumption About Pidyon Haben
General Monetary Principle: Debtor Not Obligated to Pay Before Deadline; No Assumption of Early Payment
Hamotzi Mechavero: Creditor Bears Burden of Proof; Exception When Time Has Arrived for Payment
Question: Do People Actually Pay on the Day the Debt Comes Due, or Does Chazaka Shift the Next Day?
Opening of Hired Worker Rule: Worker Swears and Collects Wages When Due (Sachir Bizmano)

topical_tags.term: Hamotzi Mechavero Alav Hara'ayah (Burden of Proof on Claimant)
Klutah K'Mi Shehunchah Damya (Object in Air as if Resting)
